2024

$9,048,000

(Source - SEC)

NHP’s IPO Plan

The company has filed an S-11 registration statement with a placeholder SEC review calculation figure of $100 million, although the final amount of the IPO may be higher.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the prospectus, as determined by the SEC.

Management said the net proceeds of the IPO will be used as follows:

We will contribute the net proceeds from this offering to the Operating Partnership in exchange for Common OP Units. We expect the Operating Partnership to use the net proceeds received from us to repay [an as-yet undetermined amount] of outstanding indebtedness under the Revolving Facility and use such remaining proceeds to fund external growth with potential future property acquisitions and for other general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC)

The company appears to be going public now to improve its balance sheet, but that may limit accretion potential in the near term for IPO investors.

The online roadshow of the company's IPO presentation is not currently available, although the roadshow only contains information in the most recent S-11 filing.

Management said the company is not the target of any legal proceedings that would be material in amount, or the threat of same.

The listed underwriters of the IPO include Wells Fargo Securities, Morgan Stanley, BMO Capital Markets, Goldman Sachs, RBC Capital Markets, Baird, Capital One Securities, Citizens Capital Markets, Fifth Third Securities, Huntington Capital Market, KeyBanc Capital Markets, Credit Agricole CIB and Synovus.

NHP Is Shifting To A Senior Housing Focus

By the end of 2025, NHP internalized management, divested some OMF assets, reduced debt and appears to be focusing future capital deployment on the higher-growth senior housing [SHOP] segment.

However, leverage remains elevated, while free cash flow is weak, and the company is still in a transition phase.

As of March 31, 2026, NHP had approximately $90.5 million in SHOP segment acquisitions ‘under letters of intent or subject to executed purchase and sale agreements.’

Its public peers have already focused on the SHOP segment, so NHP will need to increase its exposure through acquisitions as it recycles OMF capital into SHOP opportunities.

The IPO will serve to improve funding flexibility due to its immediate deleveraging result.

With competitor Janus Living's initial annual distribution yield set to 3%, NHP will likely need to match that figure to stay competitive.

The market opportunity is large, and management has correctly identified the senior housing market as having greater potential.

But, interest rates appear to be moving in the wrong direction for NHP, assuming it levers up again, such as to make a sizable acquisition.

So, while the asset strategy is improving, the balance sheet is following more slowly since the cost of capital may rise further.

For prospective investors, this IPO appears to be closer to a transition/speculative play rather than a fully de-risked compounding stock.

My overall summary of the NHP IPO so far is:

  • Positive - Industry tailwinds from strong demographic demand
  • Uncertain - Logical SHOP strategy, but execution is still in process; improving FFO but weak NOI and heavy leverage; positive deleveraging outlook, but near-term upside is limited
  • Negative - Balance sheet is heavily levered

Since the recent IPO of Janus Living was moderately well-received by investors, I suspect a similar outcome will be in play for NHP, assuming a reasonably priced IPO.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined

data.title: Tesla: Time To Dump Shares
data.description:
In the last three months since we published our recent bearish thesis on Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), the company’s stock has lost almost 20% of its value and underperformed against the market. Since that time, the situation for Tesla has only gotten worse. Shortly after we published that article, Tesla announced an annual decrease in revenue for the second year in a row. Then, at the beginning of this month, it was announced that the company’s Q1 deliveries missed expectations. As a result, we saw countless downward revisions by the analysts since there’s a risk that Tesla could once again disappoint investors and underperform in 2026.

Because of that, we continue to be pessimistic about Tesla’s future and believe that a rating of Sell makes the most sense right now.

It’s Not Getting Better

The Q1 delivery numbers that came out earlier this month were pretty disappointing. During the quarter, Tesla delivered only 358,023 vehicles, below the expectations of around 365,000 vehicles, while it also produced 408,386 cars. This indicates that while there’s no issue with producing vehicles, the demand for them is not increasing significantly. In our previous article on Tesla, we already explained that Elon Musk’s involvement in politics last year, along with the rising competition, has negatively affected Tesla’s performance, and things are unlikely to improve anytime soon.

The latest reports indicate that in Europe, Volkswagen (VWAGY) has now become the best-selling EV producer last year. At the same time, for thirteen months in a row, Tesla has experienced a decrease in registrations in Europe. Only in February did it see its first increase in registrations in a while, but there’s no guarantee that it will be able to retain the momentum in the following months due to the rise of competitors such as Volkswagen and BYD (BYDDF).

Since the automotive revenues continue to generate the absolute majority of the overall sales, there’s a risk that Tesla will once again disappoint investors this year, especially since the Q1 deliveries were below expectations. At the same time, the energy business is unlikely to offset the disappointing performance of the automotive business. This is because in Q1, deployments came in at 8.8 GWh. This is far below the 14.2 GWh record set in Q4. It’s also nowhere near the 14.4 GWh that analysts had expected. All of this makes us believe that it’s going to be extremely hard for Tesla to create additional shareholder value in the near future.

Questionable Solutions

Elon Musk’s answer to the declining car business has been to focus on autonomy and robotics. However, it’s hard to see how any advanced products are going to be quickly monetized at scale and be able to offset the underperformance of the core automotive business that’s currently focused on the selling of EVs.

In February, after eight months since its launch, Tesla’s robotaxi fleet included only around 42 vehicles. The Cybercab is expected to enter production only this month, though Musk himself has said that early volumes will be slow. Considering this, it’s hard to see how this business will be able to help turn things around for the better anytime soon. After all, CEO Elon Musk told investors back in 2019 that a million robotaxis would be on the road in 2020. Seven years after that statement was made, we’re seeing only a limited number of Tesla’s self-driving vehicles on the roads.

Then there’s Optimus. Tesla targets over $20 billion in CapEx this year, with a decent portion going toward the robot. The Fremont factory lines that used to build the Model S and Model X are being converted to produce humanoid robots instead. Last year, Musk said that 80% of Tesla’s future value will come from Optimus. We find it hard to see how the robot business turns a profit anytime soon, considering that it’s not even in the proper production stage yet. Musk may be looking a few years or even decades ahead, but the market has to deal with the business as it exists today.

In addition, Musk recently announced Terafab, which is a $25 billion chip production plant in Austin that would be a joint venture between Tesla, SpaceX (SPACE), and xAI (X.AI). It’s hard to see the project succeeding anytime soon and producing 2nm chips without previous experience in making advanced semiconductors. It took decades for TSMC (TSM) to be able to reach a point where it could start ramping up the 2nm output. That’s why it’s not a surprise that Nvidia’s (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang stated that it’s almost impossible to replicate what TSMC does when answering the question about Musk’s chip ambitions.

The financial side of Terafab is also an issue. Barclays has already noted that Terafab will push CapEx well above the $20 billion target, which Tesla guided for 2026. In addition, Morgan Stanley doesn’t expect any chips to come out of the facility before mid-2028, even under the most aggressive scenario. This is why we’re also skeptical about the ability of Tesla’s more advanced products to somehow improve the company’s current state of affairs anytime soon.

Also, all of this is happening at a time when the macro environment is experiencing a shock from the Iran war. Oil has already climbed to over $100 a barrel, while the upcoming data for March could push the inflation rate above 3%.

For Tesla specifically, higher inflation keeps the Fed from cutting rates, which means consumers are stuck with expensive financing on new vehicles. Musk made this exact point himself during 2022 and 2023. Back then, he kept saying that high interest rates were hurting Tesla because it makes it more expensive for buyers to handle the monthly payments. As such, we don’t see any upside for Tesla in the current environment.

The Real Value of Tesla

To find out Tesla’s intrinsic value in the current environment, we created a valuation model that’s available below. In the model, we used a perpetual growth rate of 3%, which is close to the historical inflation and GDP rate. The cash and debt numbers were taken from Tesla’s recent earnings report.

Tesla’s discount rate in our model is 10.85%. We figured it out by calculating the company’s cost of debt and cost of equity. In the cost of debt calculation, we used mostly Tesla’s TTM numbers. In the cost of equity valuation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.30%, a beta of 1.88, and a market return rate of 7.84%.

In January, when we were creating our previous model, our sales assumptions were close to the overall consensus at that time. Back then, the analysts estimated that Tesla’s sales growth rate in 2026 and 2027 would be 14.06% and 17.93%, respectively. After the release of the Q4 report and the announcement of Q1 deliveries, the consensus now is that the growth rate in 2026 and 2027 will only be 8.62% and 16.90%, respectively. Only closer to the terminal year the growth rate is expected to meaningfully improve. Considering this, our sales assumptions right now are similar to the latest revised consensus.

We also expect only a gradual improvement of the EBIT margin in the following years. However, the margin itself could be much lower due to the potential increase in costs, due to the increased spending on advanced products that we discussed earlier. The tax rate in the model is 21%, which is what the current corporate tax rate in the United States is. The CapEx assumptions have been increased to over $20 billion in 2026, which is what the management guided in the recent earnings call. As a result of this, the D&A assumptions have also been increased. The assumptions for the change in net working capital are similar to Tesla’s historical performance.

Our model shows that under those assumptions, Tesla’s enterprise value is $258.79 billion, while its equity value is $288.13 billion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $81.41 per share. For a company that trades at around 170 times its forward earnings, we believe that it is a fairly reasonable intrinsic value.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

Despite all of those risks, we think it’s fair to point out what could go right. As we’ve already mentioned, the Cybercab production is getting underway this month, while the robotaxi service is expected to be expanded to other cities in the foreseeable future. If we see the unsupervised FSD approved in multiple states, the revenue opportunity from ride-hailing could be meaningful over the long run.

Tesla’s performance in China is also improving. In the last two months, EV sales from the Shanghai factory were up Y/Y. Although that’s mostly because February 2025 was abysmal during the Model Y changeover. Still, the refreshed Model Y seems to be gaining traction.

Tesla also has a decent balance sheet right now. The company finished 2025 with $44.06 billion in cash and investments. That is enough to absorb a few bad quarters without financial distress. On the macro side, the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down Trump’s tariffs removed one overhang, even though a 10% replacement tariff partially offset the benefit. If oil prices come down and the Middle East situation stabilizes, some of the current macro headwinds could ease. This could push Tesla’s stock higher and slightly undermine our bearish thesis.

Final Thoughts

Although there’s a possibility that Tesla’s shares will be able to bounce in the short term, we still believe that the long-term upside is simply not there. For the last two years, the company has experienced a decline in revenues. Its Q1 deliveries were below expectations, while the performance of the energy business was also below the consensus. The solutions that are being proposed, like the development of a chip factory or the bet on full autonomy, are unlikely to be materialized anytime soon. As such, we believe that giving Tesla’s stock a rating of Sell makes the most sense at the current price.
